questions.question: What is TypeScript best described as?

options.option: A statically typed superset of JavaScript that compiles to plain JavaScript
options.comment: TypeScript extends JS with optional types. All valid JavaScript is valid TypeScript — it is a strict superset.

options.option: A JavaScript runtime like Node.js that adds native type support
A JavaScript framework for building component-based user interfaces
A compiled systems programming language inspired by JavaScript syntax

questions.question: Which company created and maintains TypeScript?

options.option: Microsoft
options.comment: TypeScript was created by Anders Hejlsberg at Microsoft in 2012 and is maintained as an open-source project on GitHub.

options.option: Google
Meta (Facebook)
JetBrains

questions.question: What file extension do TypeScript source files use?
questions.answer: .ts for regular TypeScript files, and .tsx for TypeScript files that contain JSX syntax.
questions.hint: Compare with JavaScript which uses .js and .jsx

questions.question: What command compiles a TypeScript file named app.ts?
questions.answer: tsc app.ts
questions.hint: The TypeScript compiler CLI is named after its initials

questions.question: Which of these correctly adds a type annotation to a variable in TypeScript?

options.option: let name: string = 'Alice'
options.comment: TypeScript uses colon syntax: variableName: type. The annotation goes after the variable name.

options.option: let name = string 'Alice'
let(string) name = 'Alice'
string name = 'Alice'

questions.question: What is TypeScript's relationship to JavaScript?

options.option: TypeScript is a superset — all valid JavaScript is also valid TypeScript
options.comment: Any .js file can be renamed to .ts and TypeScript will accept it. TypeScript only adds features on top.

options.option: TypeScript is a subset — it removes unsafe JavaScript features
TypeScript and JavaScript have incompatible syntax and cannot share code
TypeScript replaces JavaScript and cannot run directly in browsers

questions.question: What happens to TypeScript type annotations when compiled to JavaScript?
questions.answer: They are completely removed. TypeScript types are erased at compile time and have zero presence at runtime.
questions.hint: This process is called type erasure

questions.question: When does TypeScript check for type errors?

options.option: At compile time, before the code runs
options.comment: TypeScript's type system is purely compile-time. It checks types when you run tsc and disappears at runtime.

options.option: Only at runtime when values are actually accessed
Only when unit tests execute typed code paths
Continuously in the background via a long-running process

questions.question: What is ts-node and how does it differ from running tsc then node?
questions.answer: ts-node compiles and executes TypeScript in one step without writing .js files to disk. The tsc then node approach is two steps: compile first, then execute the generated JavaScript.
questions.hint: Think about development convenience — one command vs two

questions.question: TypeScript's type system is described as gradual. What does this mean?

options.option: You can add types incrementally — untyped code is still valid TypeScript
options.comment: The any type acts as an escape hatch, enabling file-by-file migration of large JavaScript codebases.

options.option: TypeScript becomes stricter automatically as the codebase grows
Type information is resolved gradually at runtime as values are used
Types are only checked when you opt in with a @typecheck comment

questions.question: What is the TypeScript Playground used for?

options.option: A browser-based editor at typescriptlang.org showing JS output and errors
options.comment: The Playground lets you experiment with TypeScript, explore compiler options, and share reproducible examples via URL.

options.option: A local CLI tool for scaffolding new TypeScript projects
A VS Code extension for interactive type exploration
A sandboxed Node.js environment where TypeScript runs natively

questions.question: What does structurally typed mean for TypeScript assignability?

options.option: A value is assignable to a type if it has at least the required properties
options.comment: Structural typing (duck typing): shape determines compatibility, not the declared type name. Extra properties are fine.

options.option: Types must share the exact same name declaration to be compatible
Values can only be assigned to a type if explicitly cast with as
Compatibility is checked only through class inheritance chains

questions.question: What is the key difference between TypeScript and Babel when handling TS files?

options.option: TypeScript checks types and emits JS; Babel strips types without checking
options.comment: Babel transforms TypeScript syntax but skips type checking entirely — faster builds but no type safety guarantee.

options.option: Babel performs full type checking while TypeScript only transpiles syntax
TypeScript only targets CommonJS; Babel adds ESM support on top
Babel produces better optimised output by resolving types at compile time

questions.question: What is a .d.ts file in TypeScript?
questions.answer: A declaration file containing only type definitions with no executable code. It describes the shape of an existing JavaScript library so TypeScript can type-check code that uses it.
questions.hint: The d stands for declaration. These ship alongside compiled .js files.

questions.question: What command installs TypeScript globally via npm?
questions.answer: npm install -g typescript
questions.hint: Use the -g flag to make tsc available system-wide from any directory

questions.question: What is the primary design philosophy behind TypeScript's type system?

options.option: Model real JavaScript runtime behavior, even if it means sacrificing soundness
options.comment: TypeScript is intentionally unsound in places to stay useful for real JS patterns like dynamic property access.

options.option: Achieve full soundness so every compile-safe program is also runtime-safe
Mirror the type system of Java and C# as closely as possible
Eliminate all type annotations through complete inference

questions.question: What is control flow analysis in TypeScript?
questions.answer: TypeScript tracks which types are possible at each point in code based on conditional branches, returns, and throws — automatically narrowing the type within each branch.
questions.hint: This is why TypeScript knows x is a string inside if (typeof x === 'string')

questions.question: By default, what does tsc do when it encounters type errors?

options.option: It reports the errors but still emits the JavaScript output files
options.comment: TypeScript emits output even with type errors by default. Set noEmitOnError: true to prevent JS output on errors.

options.option: It halts immediately and produces no output files
It emits JavaScript only for error-free files, silently skipping others
It writes output with inline comment markers for each type error

questions.question: What is the distinction between the target and lib compiler options in tsconfig.json?
questions.answer: target sets which JavaScript syntax version TypeScript compiles down to (affects emitted code). lib tells TypeScript which built-in APIs exist in the environment — used only for type checking, not for code output.
questions.hint: target changes the .js output; lib only changes what the type checker knows about available APIs

questions.question: What does the noEmit: true tsconfig option do?

options.option: Runs type checking without writing any JavaScript output files
options.comment: noEmit is used in monorepos or CI where a separate bundler handles output. TypeScript only acts as a type checker.

options.option: Prevents TypeScript from logging errors to the terminal
Stops TypeScript from generating declaration .d.ts files only
Disables source map generation in production builds

questions.question: What file is used to configure the TypeScript compiler for a project?
questions.answer: tsconfig.json
questions.hint: The file name combines ts (TypeScript), config (configuration), and .json

questions.question: What command generates a starter tsconfig.json file?
questions.answer: tsc --init
questions.hint: Use the TypeScript compiler with an initialization flag

questions.question: What does the target option in tsconfig.json control?

options.option: Which ECMAScript version TypeScript compiles the output JavaScript to
options.comment: target: ES2015 means arrow functions and classes stay in output. target: ES5 means they are transformed/polyfilled.

options.option: Which browsers are listed as supported by the project
Which TypeScript language features are enabled for the project
Which Node.js version the compiled output will run on

questions.question: What does the outDir compiler option specify?

options.option: The directory where TypeScript writes the compiled JavaScript files
options.comment: Without outDir, .js files are emitted next to their .ts source files. outDir keeps output separate, e.g. ./dist.

options.option: The root directory TypeScript searches for source files
The folder where TypeScript stores its internal build cache
The path where declaration .d.ts files are written separately

questions.question: What does enabling strict: true in tsconfig.json do?

options.option: Enables a group of strict type-checking flags all at once
options.comment: strict enables: strictNullChecks, noImplicitAny, strictFunctionTypes, strictPropertyInitialization, and others.

options.option: Makes all type annotations required — inference is completely disabled
Prevents the use of the any type throughout the entire project
Enables JavaScript strict mode in the emitted runtime output

questions.question: What does the rootDir option in tsconfig.json specify?
questions.answer: The root directory of the TypeScript source files. TypeScript uses it to mirror the source directory structure inside outDir.
questions.hint: Setting rootDir to ./src means ./src/index.ts compiles to ./dist/index.js when outDir is ./dist

questions.question: What does the include array in tsconfig.json control?

options.option: Which files and folders TypeScript includes in the compilation
options.comment: include supports glob patterns like ['src/**/*.ts']. Without it, TypeScript includes all .ts files in the project.

options.option: Which npm packages TypeScript includes in type checking
Which files TypeScript treats as declaration files .d.ts only
Which paths TypeScript adds to the module resolution search list

questions.question: What does strictNullChecks: true enforce in TypeScript?

options.option: null and undefined are not assignable to other types unless explicitly added
options.comment: Without strictNullChecks, null and undefined can be assigned to any type. With it you must explicitly handle them.

options.option: Variables cannot be assigned null or undefined at all in the codebase
All function arguments must have explicit null checks before use
Strict equality === is required whenever comparing to null

questions.question: What does noImplicitAny: true enforce?

options.option: TypeScript errors when it cannot infer a type and would default to any
options.comment: noImplicitAny forces you to annotate function parameters TypeScript cannot infer — preventing silent any types.

options.option: The any type is completely forbidden and cannot be written anywhere
Implicit type conversions between primitives are disallowed
All function return types must be explicitly annotated

questions.question: What is the purpose of baseUrl in tsconfig.json?
questions.answer: It sets the base directory for resolving non-relative module imports, enabling short imports like 'import { X } from "components/Button"' instead of long relative paths with ../.
questions.hint: Setting baseUrl to ./src lets you import from the src root without ../../ chains

questions.question: What is the paths compiler option used for?

options.option: Defining import path aliases that map to specific directories
options.comment: paths maps aliases like @/components/* to ['src/components/*'], enabling clean imports throughout the project.

options.option: Listing all directories where TypeScript searches for modules
Specifying which paths should be excluded from type checking
Configuring URL paths for HTTP imports in Deno-style TypeScript

questions.question: What does esModuleInterop: true fix in TypeScript?

options.option: Allows default imports from CommonJS modules without namespace syntax
options.comment: Without it you write 'import * as React from "react"'. With it, 'import React from "react"' works correctly.

options.option: Enables mixing ES modules and CommonJS files in the same project freely
Allows TypeScript to emit both ESM and CJS output simultaneously
Fixes interoperability between TypeScript and vanilla JavaScript files

questions.question: What does declaration: true in tsconfig.json produce?
questions.answer: TypeScript generates a .d.ts declaration file alongside each compiled .js file. This is required when publishing a library so consumers get full type information.
questions.hint: Essential for npm packages — the .d.ts file is what TypeScript reads when importing your library

questions.question: What does sourceMap: true enable in TypeScript?

options.option: Generates .js.map files so debuggers map errors to original TypeScript source
options.comment: Source maps let browser and Node.js debuggers show original .ts lines instead of compiled JS when errors occur.

options.option: Creates a visual map of all imported modules in the project
Enables source file watching for automatic recompilation on change
Produces a dependency graph showing which files import which

questions.question: What is incremental: true compilation in TypeScript?
questions.answer: TypeScript saves a build information file (.tsbuildinfo) after each compilation and only recompiles files that changed, making subsequent builds significantly faster.
questions.hint: Think about how it avoids recompiling unchanged files on every build

questions.question: What is the purpose of composite: true in TypeScript project references?

options.option: Marks a project as a buildable sub-project that others can reference
options.comment: composite requires declaration: true and rootDir settings, enabling tsc -b to build referenced projects incrementally.

options.option: Combines multiple tsconfig files into one merged configuration object
Enables TypeScript to output composite (bundled) JavaScript files
Creates composite types by merging multiple interface declarations

questions.question: What problem does isolatedModules: true guard against?

options.option: Flags code that cannot be safely transpiled one file at a time like Babel does
options.comment: Tools like Babel process files individually. isolatedModules errors on const enums and type-only re-exports they cannot handle.

options.option: Prevents modules from accidentally accessing each other's private state
Ensures each module runs in an isolated JavaScript VM context
Forces each module to have its own separate tsconfig.json file

questions.question: What is a TypeScript project reference (the references array in tsconfig.json)?
questions.answer: A way to split a large TypeScript project into smaller sub-projects that reference each other. TypeScript builds them incrementally in dependency order using tsc -b, improving build times in monorepos.
questions.hint: Used in monorepos where frontend, backend, and shared packages each have their own tsconfig

questions.question: Which strict mode flag ensures class properties are definitely assigned in the constructor?

options.option: strictPropertyInitialization
options.comment: With strictPropertyInitialization, class properties must be assigned in the constructor or use a definite assignment assertion (!).

options.option: strictNullChecks
strictBindCallApply
noImplicitAny

questions.question: What does skipLibCheck: true do and why is it commonly used?
questions.answer: It skips type checking of all .d.ts declaration files including those in node_modules. Commonly used to avoid errors caused by conflicting or outdated @types packages in dependencies.
questions.hint: Useful when third-party type definitions have errors you cannot fix yourself

questions.question: What does moduleResolution: node16 enable compared to the classic node strategy?

options.option: Full support for Node.js ESM resolution including .js extensions in imports
options.comment: node16 and nodenext require explicit .js extensions in ESM imports and respect package.json exports maps correctly.

options.option: Restricts TypeScript imports to Node.js built-in modules only
Enables importing from URLs as used in Deno environments
Resolves modules only from node_modules and ignores relative paths

questions.question: Which of these is NOT one of TypeScript's primitive types?

options.option: object
options.comment: object is a non-primitive type. TypeScript's primitives are: string, number, boolean, bigint, symbol, null, and undefined.

options.option: string
boolean
number

questions.question: How do you declare a typed array of numbers in TypeScript?
questions.answer: number[] or Array<number> — both are equivalent.
questions.hint: TypeScript supports two syntax forms for typed arrays

questions.question: What is the difference between null and undefined in TypeScript with strictNullChecks enabled?

options.option: They are distinct types — null is intentionally empty, undefined means unassigned
options.comment: With strictNullChecks, null and undefined are separate non-assignable types. null is explicit absence; undefined means not yet set.

options.option: They are fully interchangeable and always assignable to each other
undefined is a valid value but null is forbidden in TypeScript
Both compile away to false in the generated JavaScript output

questions.question: What does the any type do in TypeScript?

options.option: Disables all type checking for that value — any operation is allowed
options.comment: any opts out of the type system entirely. It can be assigned to and from any other type without an error.

options.option: Creates a union of all possible types currently defined in the project
Represents an intentionally empty value similar to null
Allows only primitive types like string, number, and boolean

questions.question: What is a union type in TypeScript and how is it written?
questions.answer: A union type means a value can be one of several types. Written with the pipe operator: string | number means the value can be a string or a number.
questions.hint: Use the | operator between the types you want to allow

questions.question: What is a TypeScript tuple?

options.option: A typed array with a fixed number of elements where each position has a known type
options.comment: Example: [string, number] is a tuple with exactly two elements — first a string, then a number.

options.option: A special object type with mixed-type key-value pairs
A read-only array that cannot be mutated after creation
An array that automatically infers types for each index from usage

questions.question: What is a TypeScript enum?

options.option: A way to define a set of named constants with numeric or string values
options.comment: Enums group related named values, like Direction.Up = 0 or Status.Active = 'ACTIVE', making intent clear.

options.option: A type that can only hold undefined or null values
A special array type that enumerates its allowed element types
A TypeScript version of JavaScript's Symbol primitive

questions.question: What is the void type used for in TypeScript?
questions.answer: void is the return type for functions that do not return a meaningful value — they either return undefined or have no return statement.
questions.hint: It is the type-level equivalent of a function that exists only for its side effects

questions.question: What is the difference between unknown and any in TypeScript?

options.option: unknown is type-safe: you must narrow it before using it; any skips all checks
options.comment: You cannot call methods or access properties on unknown without a type guard first. any bypasses all such checks.

options.option: unknown allows any operation; any requires explicit casting before use
unknown is only for runtime values; any is only for compile-time generics
unknown is deprecated; any is the recommended type-safe escape hatch

questions.question: What is the never type in TypeScript?

options.option: The type of values that can never occur — functions that always throw or loop forever
options.comment: never is assignable to every type, but no type is assignable to never. It marks unreachable code and exhaustive checks.

options.option: A type alias for the absence of a return value, equivalent to void
A placeholder type for an unknown value that will be resolved later
The inferred type for untyped variables with no assignment

questions.question: What is a const enum and how does it differ from a regular enum?

options.option: const enum values are inlined at compile time — no enum object is emitted
options.comment: Regular enums create a JS object at runtime. const enums are fully replaced by their literal values in output — smaller code.

options.option: const enum prevents enum values from being reassigned at runtime
const enum requires all members to have explicitly assigned numeric values
const enum values can be used in runtime switch statements only

questions.question: What is type widening in TypeScript?
questions.answer: When TypeScript infers a broader type than the literal value. For example, let x = 'hello' infers string (widened) rather than the literal type 'hello'.
questions.hint: Compare let vs const — const keeps the literal type because the value cannot change

questions.question: What is type narrowing in TypeScript?

options.option: Refining a broad type to a more specific one within a conditional branch
options.comment: After typeof x === 'string', TypeScript knows x is a string in that branch — narrowed from string | number.

options.option: Reducing the number of union members using Exclude<T, U>
Converting a wider type to a narrower one using the as keyword
Removing optional properties from an interface to make it stricter

questions.question: What is a string enum and when is it preferred over a numeric enum?
questions.answer: A string enum assigns string values to each member, e.g. enum Direction { Up = 'UP' }. It is preferred when values need to be human-readable in logs, API payloads, or databases — unlike numeric enums whose values are meaningless integers.
questions.hint: Think about what you see when you serialize or log an enum value

questions.question: What does the readonly modifier do when applied to an array type?

options.option: Prevents mutating methods like push, pop, and splice from being called
options.comment: readonly T[] (or ReadonlyArray<T>) disables all mutating array methods at the type level. The array cannot be changed.

options.option: Creates a deep freeze so nested objects are also immutable
Makes the array variable itself constant — it cannot be reassigned
Prevents array elements from being iterated with for-of loops

questions.question: When should you prefer unknown over any for values from external sources?

options.option: Always — unknown forces you to narrow the type before use, preventing silent bugs
options.comment: Using unknown for API responses or JSON.parse results forces explicit type guards, making the code intentionally safe.

options.option: Only when the value could be a complex object — any is fine for primitives
Only inside generic functions — any is preferred for regular typed variables
Never — unknown is an internal type not intended for user-written code

questions.question: What is an intersection type and how does it differ from a union type?
questions.answer: An intersection type (A & B) combines multiple types into one — the result must satisfy ALL combined types simultaneously. A union type (A | B) means the value satisfies any ONE of the listed types.
questions.hint: Intersection = AND (must have everything); Union = OR (must have one of them)

questions.question: What is the purpose of the never type in exhaustive union checks?

options.option: Assigning to never in a default branch causes a compile error if a case is missed
options.comment: const _: never = value in a default case errors at compile time if value still has unhandled union members.

options.option: never prevents the switch statement from requiring a default case
never is the return type of switch statements without a default case
Assigning to never logs a runtime warning for unexpected values

questions.question: What is the bigint type used for in TypeScript?

options.option: Representing integers larger than Number.MAX_SAFE_INTEGER precisely
options.comment: bigint (e.g. 9007199254740993n) handles arbitrarily large integers. number loses precision past 2^53 - 1.

options.option: A 64-bit floating point type more precise than the number type
An alias for number that enforces integer-only values at runtime
A numeric type used exclusively for database IDs and financial values

questions.question: What is the difference between declaring a type alias for a union vs using it inline?
questions.answer: A type alias (type Status = 'active' | 'inactive') gives the union a reusable name and improves readability in error messages. Inline unions work but cannot be reused or documented in one place.
questions.hint: Think about maintainability — what happens when you need the same union in ten places?

questions.question: What is the symbol primitive type used for in TypeScript?

options.option: Creating unique, non-comparable values useful as guaranteed-unique keys
options.comment: Symbols are unique and immutable. Symbol('id') !== Symbol('id'). Useful for object keys that cannot accidentally conflict.

options.option: Representing mathematical symbols like pi and infinity in calculations
A type alias for string literals used as object property names
A special marker type used internally by TypeScript's compiler only

questions.question: What is an interface in TypeScript?

options.option: A named contract describing the shape an object must have
options.comment: Interfaces define object shapes with named properties and their types. They are purely a compile-time construct — no runtime object.

options.option: A class that cannot be instantiated directly
A runtime object that validates data at the point of assignment
An ES6 class with only abstract methods

questions.question: How do you mark a property as optional in a TypeScript interface?
questions.answer: Add a question mark after the property name: property?: type. For example: name?: string means name may or may not be present.
questions.hint: The ? modifier also works on type alias properties and function parameters

questions.question: What does the readonly modifier do on an interface property?

options.option: Prevents the property from being reassigned after the object is created
options.comment: readonly is a compile-time check only — it does not use Object.freeze. The property can still be mutated at runtime.

options.option: Prevents the property from being read outside the current module
Creates a hidden private backing field for the property
Marks the property as non-enumerable in JavaScript's property descriptor

questions.question: What is the key difference between interface and type alias in TypeScript?

options.option: Interfaces support declaration merging; type aliases do not
options.comment: Declaring the same interface name twice merges them. Duplicate type aliases cause an error. Both can describe object shapes.

options.option: Type aliases cannot describe object shapes — only primitives and unions
Interfaces support generics; type aliases do not
type aliases are faster at compile time than interfaces

questions.question: How do you extend one interface from another in TypeScript?
questions.answer: Use the extends keyword: interface Admin extends User { role: string }. The extending interface inherits all properties from the base interface.
questions.hint: The syntax is the same as extending a class, but interfaces have no runtime cost

questions.question: What is structural typing (duck typing) in the context of TypeScript interfaces?

options.option: An object satisfies an interface if it has all the required properties
options.comment: TypeScript checks shape, not the declared type name. An object literal with the right properties satisfies any matching interface.

options.option: An object must explicitly declare it implements an interface to satisfy it
Only class instances can satisfy an interface — plain objects cannot
Properties must be in the same order as declared in the interface

questions.question: What is declaration merging for interfaces in TypeScript?
questions.answer: When you declare an interface with the same name twice, TypeScript automatically merges both declarations into one combined interface with all properties from both declarations.
questions.hint: This is how libraries like @types/express add properties to the Request object

questions.question: What is an index signature in a TypeScript interface?

options.option: A way to type objects with dynamic string or number keys of a uniform value type
options.comment: { [key: string]: number } says any string key maps to a number value — useful for dictionary-like objects.

options.option: A way to specify which array index holds which type in a tuple
A method signature that describes how an object can be called as a function
A numeric property that tracks how many items are in a collection

questions.question: What is an intersection type in TypeScript?

options.option: A type that combines multiple types so the value must satisfy all of them
options.comment: A & B means the value must have all properties of A AND all properties of B. Often used to compose object types.

options.option: A type that picks the properties shared between two types
A type that represents the overlap between two union types
A conditional type that returns the common type when A extends B

questions.question: When should you prefer type alias over interface?

options.option: When defining union types, intersection types, or aliasing primitives
options.comment: type alias can represent union (A | B), intersection (A & B), mapped types, and primitives. interface only handles object shapes.

options.option: When you need to extend from multiple base types simultaneously
When you need declaration merging to add properties incrementally
When the type will be implemented by a class using the implements keyword

questions.question: How does TypeScript handle excess property checks on object literals?
questions.answer: TypeScript performs excess property checking on object literals assigned directly to an interface — it errors if extra properties are present. This check is skipped for variables that already have a compatible type, allowing extra properties.
questions.hint: Try passing { name: 'Alice', age: 30, extra: true } directly vs through an intermediate variable

questions.question: What does an interface extending multiple interfaces look like?

options.option: interface C extends A, B { } — comma-separated list of base interfaces
options.comment: Unlike classes, interfaces can extend multiple other interfaces in a single declaration, merging all their properties.

options.option: interface C extends A & B { } — using intersection syntax
interface C extends A extends B { } — chained extends keywords
interface C implements A, B { } — using the implements keyword

questions.question: What is a callable interface in TypeScript?
questions.answer: An interface that describes a value that can be called as a function by including a call signature: interface Formatter { (value: string): string }. The interface can also include additional properties.
questions.hint: Useful for typing functions that also have properties attached to them

questions.question: What is the difference between type alias extending and interface extending?

options.option: type alias uses & intersection; interface uses extends — both inherit properties
options.comment: type C = A & B is the type alias equivalent of interface C extends A, B. Both result in a type requiring all properties.

options.option: Only interface supports inheritance; type aliases always create flat copies
interface extends is checked lazily; type & is checked eagerly at definition
type alias & allows property overriding; interface extends does not

questions.question: What is module augmentation using interface declaration merging?
questions.answer: Re-declaring an interface from an external module inside a declare module block adds new properties to it. For example, augmenting Express's Request interface to add a user property for typed authentication middleware.
questions.hint: This lets you extend a library's types without modifying the library's own files

questions.question: Why can type aliases not be used with the implements keyword in classes?

options.option: They can — a class can implement any type alias that describes an object shape
options.comment: class Foo implements MyType is valid whether MyType is an interface or a type alias, as long as it is an object shape.

options.option: implements only works with interface declarations, not type aliases
Type aliases require the class to extend them instead of implement them
implements requires the type to be in the same file as the class

questions.question: What happens when two merged interface declarations have conflicting property types?

options.option: TypeScript raises a compile error — conflicting declarations are not allowed
options.comment: Declaration merging requires that overlapping properties have compatible types. An incompatible type causes a compile error.

options.option: The second declaration silently overrides the first property type
TypeScript infers an intersection of both conflicting types automatically
TypeScript widens the property to the any type to resolve the conflict

questions.question: When should you use interface vs type alias for defining object shapes in a shared library?
questions.answer: Prefer interface for public API shapes that consumers may need to augment via declaration merging. Use type alias for complex compositions (unions, mapped types) or when you explicitly want to prevent augmentation.
questions.hint: Library authors often expose interfaces so users can extend them; internal implementation types can be aliases

questions.question: How do you annotate the return type of a function in TypeScript?

options.option: After the closing parenthesis with a colon: function greet(): string { }
options.comment: Return types go after the parameter list with a colon. TypeScript can usually infer them, but explicit annotations are clearer.

options.option: Before the function keyword: string function greet() { }
Inside the parameter list as the last parameter: function greet(return: string) { }
With the @returns decorator above the function declaration

questions.question: How do you mark a function parameter as optional in TypeScript?
questions.answer: Add a question mark after the parameter name: function greet(name?: string). Optional parameters must come after required parameters.
questions.hint: The same ? syntax used in interfaces — inside the function the parameter has type string | undefined

questions.question: What are the TypeScript access modifiers for class members?

options.option: public, private, and protected
options.comment: public: accessible anywhere; private: class only; protected: class and subclasses. All compile away — they are type-level checks.

options.option: public, private, internal, and protected
readonly, private, and static
public, hidden, and restricted

questions.question: What does the abstract keyword on a class mean in TypeScript?

options.option: The class cannot be instantiated directly — it must be subclassed
options.comment: Abstract classes can have both abstract methods (no body, must be overridden) and concrete methods with implementation.

options.option: The class has no constructor and all properties must be set by the caller
All methods in the class are automatically private
The class is a pure interface with no method implementations allowed

questions.question: What does the implements keyword do when used on a class?
questions.answer: It declares that the class satisfies a specific interface or type alias. TypeScript will error if the class is missing any required property or method from that interface.
questions.hint: implements is a compile-time check only — it adds no runtime behavior

questions.question: What is a parameter property shorthand in TypeScript classes?

options.option: Adding an access modifier to a constructor parameter to auto-declare and assign it
options.comment: constructor(public name: string) is shorthand for declaring a name property and assigning it from the constructor argument.

options.option: A way to set default values for constructor parameters inline
An optional constructor parameter that is also a class property
A private property that is automatically derived from another property

questions.question: What are rest parameters in TypeScript and how are they typed?
questions.answer: Rest parameters collect all remaining arguments into an array. They are typed as an array: function sum(...nums: number[]): number. They must be the last parameter.
questions.hint: The three dots syntax from JavaScript — TypeScript just adds a type annotation for the collected array

questions.question: What is a function overload in TypeScript?

options.option: Multiple call signatures for a function with one implementation below them
options.comment: Overload signatures describe the valid calling patterns. The single implementation signature (wider type) handles all cases.

options.option: Two separate functions with the same name but different bodies
A function that can call itself recursively with different argument types
A decorator that replaces a method with an overridden version at runtime

questions.question: What is the this parameter annotation in TypeScript functions?

options.option: A fake first parameter that types what this refers to inside the function
options.comment: function greet(this: User, msg: string) types the required this context. It is erased from the compiled JavaScript output.

options.option: A parameter that passes the current class instance to a static method
An arrow function parameter that binds this to the outer context
A reference parameter that must be passed as the first argument by callers

questions.question: What is the difference between TypeScript's private modifier and JavaScript's # private field syntax?
questions.answer: TypeScript's private is only a compile-time check — the property is accessible at runtime. JavaScript # fields are truly private at runtime using a hard-coded WeakMap — inaccessible even via reflection.
questions.hint: One is erased at compile time, the other enforces privacy in the JavaScript runtime

questions.question: What does the readonly modifier mean on a class property?

options.option: The property can only be assigned in the constructor — not after
options.comment: readonly class properties can be set during construction but cannot be reassigned anywhere else in the class or externally.

options.option: The property is automatically frozen with Object.freeze at runtime
The property is accessible from outside the class but not modifiable
The property is private and can only be read by getter methods

questions.question: What does a static class member mean in TypeScript?

options.option: The property or method belongs to the class itself, not instances of it
options.comment: Static members are accessed via ClassName.member, not this.member. They are shared across all instances of the class.

options.option: The member is frozen and cannot be changed after the class is defined
The member is only accessible within the file where the class is defined
The member runs once when the module is first loaded, then is cached

questions.question: What is an abstract method in a TypeScript abstract class?
questions.answer: A method declared with abstract and no body. Concrete subclasses must provide an implementation. Abstract methods can only exist inside abstract classes.
questions.hint: abstract getName(): string — no curly braces, just the signature

questions.question: What is the difference between default parameters and optional parameters in TypeScript?

options.option: Default params have a fallback value; optional params become undefined if omitted
options.comment: function greet(name = 'World') vs function greet(name?: string). Default params are always a defined value inside the function.

options.option: Optional params come before required params; default params come last
Default params require the ? modifier; optional params use = value
They are identical — TypeScript treats them as the same construct internally

questions.question: What is a class expression in TypeScript?

options.option: Assigning a class definition to a variable: const Foo = class { };
options.comment: Class expressions work like function expressions — the class can be named or anonymous and assigned to a variable.

options.option: An expression that evaluates to a specific class type using as ClassName
A class that is generated at runtime by a factory function
An interface that can be used as both a type and a constructor

questions.question: How do you type a function that accepts another function as a parameter?
questions.answer: Use an inline function type annotation: function run(callback: (value: string) => void). Or define a type alias: type Handler = (value: string) => void and use that as the parameter type.
questions.hint: The arrow syntax inside the parameter type describes the expected signature of the callback

questions.question: What happens if a subclass constructor does not call super() before accessing this?

options.option: TypeScript errors at compile time — super() must be called before this in subclass constructors
options.comment: This is a JavaScript rule that TypeScript enforces. Accessing this before super() in a derived class is a runtime error.

options.option: TypeScript silently calls super() for you without any arguments
The subclass constructor is not allowed to access base class properties
The base class constructor is bypassed and not executed at all

questions.question: What is function type overloading best suited for in TypeScript?

options.option: Functions with different return types depending on what argument type is passed
options.comment: Classic example: function parse(input: string): number; function parse(input: number): string; The return type changes with input.

options.option: Functions with optional final parameters that change the function's name
Methods that are defined multiple times in different subclasses
Functions that call themselves recursively with narrowing types

questions.question: What is a getter and setter in TypeScript classes and how are they typed?
questions.answer: Getters (get prop()) and setters (set prop(value)) are accessor methods. TypeScript infers the getter return type automatically; the setter parameter type must match or be broader than the getter return type.
questions.hint: get name(): string { } and set name(value: string) { } — getter return type drives the property type

questions.question: What problem do generics solve in TypeScript?

options.option: Writing reusable typed code without losing type information by using any
options.comment: Generics allow functions and classes to work with different types while keeping full type safety on both input and output.

options.option: Automatically generating code for repetitive patterns at compile time
Creating runtime polymorphism across class hierarchies
Generating TypeScript interfaces from JSON schemas automatically

questions.question: What does the T in function identity<T>(value: T): T mean?
questions.answer: T is a type parameter — a placeholder for a specific type that TypeScript fills in when the function is called. It ensures the input and output types are the same.
questions.hint: T is just a convention; you could name it anything — Item, Value, etc.

questions.question: How does TypeScript infer the type argument when you call a generic function?

options.option: It infers T from the type of the argument passed to the function
options.comment: When you call identity('hello'), TypeScript infers T = string from the argument. You can also pass it explicitly: identity<string>('hello').

options.option: It always requires you to explicitly pass the type argument in angle brackets
It infers T from the expected return type at the call site
It defaults T to any if no argument is passed

questions.question: What does a generic constraint like T extends object do?

options.option: Restricts T to only types that are assignable to object
options.comment: T extends object means T must be a non-primitive type. You can access properties that all objects have like toString().

options.option: Forces T to be a subclass of the object class
Makes T inherit all properties from the object type automatically
Limits T to types that have been defined with the class keyword

questions.question: What is a generic interface in TypeScript? Give an example.
questions.answer: An interface with a type parameter: interface Repository<T> { findById(id: number): T; save(item: T): void }. The same interface can be used for different entity types: Repository<User>, Repository<Product>.
questions.hint: The angle brackets go after the interface name just like on functions

questions.question: What does T extends keyof U mean as a generic constraint?

options.option: T must be one of the property names (keys) of type U
options.comment: keyof U is a union of U's property names as string/number literals. T extends keyof U means T must be one of those keys.

options.option: T must be a subtype that inherits from type U
T and U must share at least one property key in common
T extends all properties of U rather than just its keys

questions.question: What does the generic function function getProperty<T, K extends keyof T>(obj: T, key: K) return?

options.option: T[K] — the type of the value at that specific key of T
options.comment: The return type T[K] is an indexed access type. It precisely types the value at key K of object T.

options.option: keyof T — the union of all key names of T
K — the type of the key itself, not the value it points to
unknown — property access always returns unknown in generic functions

questions.question: What is a generic class in TypeScript and how is it used?
questions.answer: A class with a type parameter: class Stack<T> { private items: T[] = []; push(item: T) { } pop(): T | undefined { } }. When instantiated, T is fixed: new Stack<string>() creates a type-safe string stack.
questions.hint: The type parameter on the class is shared across all methods and properties in that class

questions.question: What is a default type parameter in TypeScript generics?

options.option: A fallback type used when the type argument is not provided or inferred
options.comment: interface Response<T = unknown> uses unknown if T is not specified. Similar to a default function parameter but for types.

options.option: A type that TypeScript defaults to when an error would otherwise occur
The base type that all instances of the generic must extend
A type narrowed to the most common value passed at call sites

questions.question: What is a const type parameter (const T) introduced in TypeScript 5.0?

options.option: Infers the narrowest literal type instead of widening to a base type
options.comment: Without const, identity(['a', 'b']) infers string[]. With function identity<const T>(v: T), it infers readonly ['a', 'b'].

options.option: Prevents the type parameter from being passed explicitly at the call site
Makes the type parameter immutable so it cannot be narrowed by type guards
Requires the passed value to be a JavaScript const variable

questions.question: What does it mean to constrain a generic type parameter with T extends { length: number }?
questions.answer: It means T must be a type that has a length property of type number. This constraint lets the function safely access value.length without TypeScript erroring. Strings, arrays, and typed arrays all satisfy this.
questions.hint: Any type with a numeric length property qualifies — you are not tied to arrays or strings specifically

questions.question: What happens when TypeScript cannot infer a type argument and no default is provided?

options.option: TypeScript defaults the type parameter to unknown (for safe generic functions)
options.comment: When TypeScript cannot infer T from usage context and no default is given, T resolves to unknown — the safe fallback.

options.option: TypeScript defaults to any, disabling type checking for that call
TypeScript produces a compile error requiring an explicit type argument
TypeScript defaults to the constraint type if one is specified, otherwise never

questions.question: What is the difference between a generic function and a function that takes an any parameter?
questions.answer: A generic function preserves the relationship between input and output types — T makes the return type match the input type. A function with any loses that relationship: any input gives any output, with no type safety preserved.
questions.hint: With generics, if you pass a string you get a string back — with any, you get any back

questions.question: What is a higher-order generic function in TypeScript?

options.option: A function that takes or returns another generic function, passing type parameters through
options.comment: Example: function wrap<T>(fn: (x: T) => T): (x: T) => T passes the type parameter from the outer to the inner function.

options.option: A function with more than one generic type parameter
A generic function that calls itself recursively with narrower types
A class method that inherits the class's generic type parameter

questions.question: What does multiple type parameters look like and when is it useful?

options.option: function pair<A, B>(a: A, b: B): [A, B] — types two independent values
options.comment: Multiple type parameters model functions that work on two independently typed values, like key-value pairs or zip operations.

options.option: function pair<A extends B>(a: A, b: B) — A inherits from B's type
function pair<A, A>(a: A, b: A) — two parameters of the same inferred type
function pair<A | B>(val: A | B) — a union of two type parameters

questions.question: What is the variance of a generic type in TypeScript — when is it covariant or contravariant?
questions.answer: Covariant: if T extends U, then Container<T> is assignable to Container<U> (output positions like return types). Contravariant: if T extends U, Container<U> is assignable to Container<T> (input positions like function parameters).
questions.hint: Return types are covariant (more specific is fine); parameter types are contravariant (more general is required)

questions.question: What is a literal type in TypeScript?

options.option: A type that represents one specific value, like 'success' or 42
options.comment: Literal types narrow a type to an exact value. 'success' is a type matching only that string. Useful in discriminated unions.

options.option: A type that represents JavaScript template literal syntax
A type annotated with a specific runtime value using as const
A string, number, or boolean type annotated verbatim in source code

questions.question: What is a discriminated union (tagged union) in TypeScript?

options.option: A union where each member has a shared literal property used to distinguish it
options.comment: Example: { kind: 'circle'; radius: number } | { kind: 'square'; side: number } — TypeScript narrows by checking kind.

options.option: A union type that excludes certain members using Exclude<T, U>
A union that restricts assignment based on type guard return values
A union of class types where discrimination is by instanceof check

questions.question: What is a type predicate (user-defined type guard) in TypeScript?
questions.answer: A function with a return type of the form 'param is Type'. When it returns true, TypeScript narrows the parameter to that type. Example: function isString(x: unknown): x is string { return typeof x === 'string'; }
questions.hint: The is keyword in the return type is what makes TypeScript trust this function as a type guard

questions.question: Which of these is a valid type guard that narrows a union type?

options.option: The in operator: if ('radius' in shape) { /* shape is Circle here */ }
options.comment: The in operator checks property existence and narrows the type. TypeScript uses it as a control flow narrowing mechanism.

options.option: The typeof operator applied to objects: typeof shape === 'Circle'
A direct equality check on the variable: if (shape === Circle) { }
Calling a built-in isType() function from the TypeScript standard library

questions.question: What is the satisfies operator introduced in TypeScript 4.9?

options.option: Validates a value matches a type without widening the inferred type
options.comment: const config = { port: 3000 } satisfies ServerConfig — validates against the type but keeps the literal type 3000, not number.

options.option: Asserts that a value is of a specific type, similar to as
Checks at runtime whether a value satisfies an interface constraint
An alternative to implements for checking class shapes at the type level

questions.question: What is a conditional type in TypeScript?

options.option: A type that resolves differently based on whether T extends U: T extends U ? X : Y
options.comment: Conditional types work like ternary expressions at the type level. They are evaluated lazily and distribute over unions.

options.option: A type that only exists when a certain compiler flag is enabled
A type that TypeScript computes differently in strict vs non-strict mode
A union type that conditionally includes or excludes members at runtime

questions.question: What does the infer keyword do inside a conditional type?
questions.answer: infer declares a new type variable that TypeScript fills in by pattern matching during the conditional check. Example: T extends Promise<infer U> ? U : never extracts the type inside a Promise.
questions.hint: Think of infer as a capture group in a regex — it extracts part of the matched type

questions.question: What is a mapped type in TypeScript?

options.option: A type that transforms all properties of another type by iterating its keys
options.comment: { [K in keyof T]: T[K] } is the identity mapped type. You can modify each property — make them optional, readonly, etc.

options.option: A type created by calling .map() on a tuple or union type
A type that maps string keys to a fixed value type, like Record<K, V>
A way to rename type parameters in generic functions

questions.question: What are template literal types introduced in TypeScript 4.1?

options.option: Types that compose string literal types using template literal syntax
options.comment: type EventName<T extends string> = `on${Capitalize<T>}` — creates 'onClick', 'onChange', etc. from 'click', 'change'.

options.option: A way to embed type expressions inside string template literals at runtime
String types with embedded regular expressions for pattern matching
Compile-time macros that generate TypeScript code from string patterns

questions.question: What is distributive behavior of conditional types over union types?
questions.answer: When a conditional type has a naked (unwrapped) type parameter on the left side of extends, it distributes over each member of a union. IsString<string | number> becomes IsString<string> | IsString<number>.
questions.hint: Naked T extends X ? Y : Z distributes; wrapped [T] extends [X] ? Y : Z does not

questions.question: What does the keyof operator return in TypeScript?

options.option: A union of all property name types of an object type
options.comment: keyof { name: string; age: number } = 'name' | 'age'. For arrays and tuples, it includes numeric indices too.

options.option: An array of runtime property names extracted from an object instance
The number of keys in the type — a numeric type literal
A type that matches any key of the given object regardless of type

questions.question: What is an indexed access type in TypeScript?

options.option: T[K] — looks up the type of property K within type T
options.comment: User['name'] gives the type of the name property of User. Works with keyof to build utility types.

options.option: A type that accesses the runtime value of an indexed property
Accessing an array element type using its numeric position
A mapped type modifier that makes each property an array type

questions.question: What is the difference between as const and satisfies?
questions.answer: as const widens nothing and makes the inferred type as narrow as possible (readonly literals). satisfies validates the value against a given type without widening — it keeps narrow types but also checks structure. Both can be combined: value satisfies Type as const.
questions.hint: as const is about inference width; satisfies is about validation against a contract

questions.question: How do you create a homomorphic mapped type that removes the readonly modifier from all properties?

options.option: { -readonly [K in keyof T]: T[K] } — the minus prefix removes the modifier
options.comment: Prefix - removes modifiers; prefix + adds them. -readonly removes readonly; -? removes optional on all properties.

options.option: { [K in keyof T]: Required<T[K]> } — wrapping each property in Required
{ [K in keyof T] as Mutable<K>: T[K] } — using a key remapping clause
Mapped types cannot remove modifiers — you need a utility type for that

questions.question: What does the as keyword do inside a mapped type (key remapping)?

options.option: Renames or filters keys in the mapped type using a type expression
options.comment: { [K in keyof T as `get${Capitalize<string & K>}`]: () => T[K] } remaps each key to a getter name using template literals.

options.option: Casts each property value to the specified type
Exports the mapped type under a different name in the module
Applies as const to each mapped property automatically

questions.question: What is an assertion function in TypeScript and how does it differ from a type predicate?
questions.answer: An assertion function uses asserts param is Type in its return type annotation. If it returns normally (without throwing), TypeScript narrows the type from that point on. A type predicate returns a boolean; an assertion function returns void and throws on failure.
questions.hint: Example: function assert(x: unknown): asserts x is string { if (typeof x !== 'string') throw new Error(); }

questions.question: What is the purpose of the NoInfer<T> utility type added in TypeScript 5.4?

options.option: Prevents TypeScript from inferring T from a specific argument position
options.comment: NoInfer<T> excludes an argument from the inference sites TypeScript considers, forcing T to be inferred from other arguments.

options.option: Marks a type as having no inferrable information — equivalent to unknown
Prevents a type parameter from being passed explicitly at the call site
Removes all inference caching for a type to force re-evaluation

questions.question: What does Partial<T> do in TypeScript?

options.option: Makes all properties of T optional
options.comment: Partial<User> produces { name?: string; age?: number } — every property becomes optional. Useful for update/patch payloads.

options.option: Picks a subset of properties from T
Makes all properties of T required even if originally optional
Creates a partial deep clone of T at runtime

questions.question: What does Readonly<T> produce?

options.option: A type where all properties of T are readonly and cannot be reassigned
options.comment: Readonly<User> adds readonly to every property. It is a compile-time check only — Object.freeze is needed at runtime.

options.option: A type that deep-freezes nested objects and arrays recursively
A type where the variable binding itself cannot be reassigned
A type alias that removes all optional properties from T

questions.question: What does Pick<T, K> do and when is it useful?
questions.answer: Pick<T, K> creates a new type with only the properties K selected from T. Useful when building a subset view of an entity — like a DTO with only the fields you want to expose.
questions.hint: K must be one or more keys of T — use union notation: Pick<User, 'name' | 'email'>

questions.question: What does Omit<T, K> do?
questions.answer: Omit<T, K> creates a new type with all properties of T except those listed in K. It is the inverse of Pick — useful for removing sensitive or system-generated fields like id or createdAt from a DTO.
questions.hint: Think of it as Pick everything except K

questions.question: What does Record<K, V> create?

options.option: An object type where each key of K maps to a value of type V
options.comment: Record<string, number> is equivalent to { [key: string]: number }. Record<'a' | 'b', boolean> forces both keys to exist.

options.option: A runtime Map from keys of type K to values of type V
A tuple with one key and one value of the specified types
A type that transforms each property name to the type K and its value to V

questions.question: What does Required<T> do?

options.option: Makes all optional properties of T required
options.comment: Required<Partial<User>> undoes Partial — removes the ? from every property. Useful when validating a complete object.

options.option: Adds readonly to every property of T
Removes null and undefined from every property of T
Marks the type itself as required when used as a function parameter

questions.question: What does ReturnType<F> extract?

options.option: The return type of the function type F
options.comment: ReturnType<typeof fetch> gives the return type of the fetch function. Very useful for typing values returned by factory functions.

options.option: The type of the first parameter of function F
Whether F returns void, undefined, or a value type
The async return type — it unwraps Promise<T> to T automatically

questions.question: What does Parameters<F> extract from a function type?
questions.answer: Parameters<F> extracts the parameter types of function type F as a tuple. Parameters<(a: string, b: number) => void> gives [string, number].
questions.hint: Useful when you want to re-use a function's parameter signature in another function

questions.question: What is the difference between Extract<T, U> and Exclude<T, U>?

options.option: Extract keeps members of T assignable to U; Exclude removes them
options.comment: Extract<'a'|'b'|'c', 'a'|'c'> = 'a'|'c'. Exclude<'a'|'b'|'c', 'a'|'c'> = 'b'. Opposites of each other.

options.option: Extract picks properties by name; Exclude removes them from an object type
Extract is for union types; Exclude is for object property types only
They are equivalent — Extract<T, U> equals Exclude<T, Exclude<T, U>>

questions.question: What does NonNullable<T> produce?

options.option: Removes null and undefined from the union type T
options.comment: NonNullable<string | null | undefined> = string. Equivalent to Exclude<T, null | undefined>.

options.option: Removes all falsy types (null, undefined, 0, '') from T
Makes every property of object type T required and non-optional
Transforms T to disallow null assignment at runtime

questions.question: What does Awaited<T> do and when was it added?
questions.answer: Awaited<T> recursively unwraps the resolved type of a Promise (or promise-like). Awaited<Promise<Promise<string>>> = string. Added in TypeScript 4.5.
questions.hint: It handles nested promises — Awaited<Promise<string>> = string, not the inner Promise

questions.question: What does InstanceType<T> extract?

options.option: The instance type produced by calling new on constructor type T
options.comment: InstanceType<typeof MyClass> gives the type of an instance of MyClass. Useful when working with class constructors as values.

options.option: The static type of the class T, not the instance
The prototype object type of constructor T
The type of the first argument passed to the constructor of T

questions.question: How do you implement Partial<T> from scratch using mapped types?
questions.answer: type MyPartial<T> = { [K in keyof T]?: T[K] }. The ? in the mapped type makes each property optional. This is exactly how the built-in Partial<T> is implemented in TypeScript's lib.
questions.hint: A mapped type that adds ? to every key of T

questions.question: How would you implement a DeepPartial<T> utility type?

options.option: { [K in keyof T]?: T[K] extends object ? DeepPartial<T[K]> : T[K] }
options.comment: DeepPartial recurses into object properties. Without the conditional check, it only makes the top level optional.

options.option: Partial<T> already handles nested objects — no custom type needed
{ [K in keyof T]: Partial<T[K]> } — apply Partial to each value type
Recursive utility types are not supported — TypeScript prevents infinite types

questions.question: What does ConstructorParameters<T> extract?

options.option: The parameter types of a class constructor as a tuple
options.comment: ConstructorParameters<typeof Date> gives the arguments Date's constructor accepts. Useful for forwarding args to new T().

options.option: The return type of the class constructor — same as InstanceType<T>
The number of parameters the constructor requires at runtime
The generic type parameters the class T was constructed with

questions.question: How do you chain utility types to build a create DTO that omits id and createdAt, and makes all remaining fields optional?
questions.answer: Partial<Omit<User, 'id' | 'createdAt'>>. First Omit removes the unwanted fields, then Partial makes everything else optional. Utility types compose naturally because each produces a new type.
questions.hint: Apply the outer utility type to the result of the inner one

questions.question: What does the Uppercase<S> intrinsic string utility type do?

options.option: Converts a string literal type to its uppercase equivalent
options.comment: Uppercase<'hello'> = 'HELLO'. These intrinsic types (Uppercase, Lowercase, Capitalize, Uncapitalize) work only on string literals.

options.option: Ensures the runtime string value is converted to uppercase at assignment
Maps each character of a string type to its ASCII code number
An alias for string that enforces uppercase-only values at runtime

questions.question: How is Omit<T, K> implemented internally in TypeScript?

options.option: Pick<T, Exclude<keyof T, K>> — pick all keys except those in K
options.comment: Omit is implemented as Pick of all keys except K. Understanding this helps you build similar custom utility types.

options.option: { [P in keyof T]: P extends K ? never : T[P] } — sets excluded keys to never
T & { [P in K]: never } — intersects T with an object making K properties never
Exclude<T, K> — removing keys from T the same way Exclude removes union members

questions.question: What are the two types of exports in a TypeScript module?
questions.answer: Named exports (export const x = ...) and a default export (export default ...). A module can have multiple named exports but only one default export.
questions.hint: Named exports are imported by their original name (or aliased); default export is imported without braces

questions.question: What makes a TypeScript file a module (as opposed to a global script)?

options.option: The presence of at least one import or export statement
options.comment: A file without any import/export is a script — its declarations are globally visible. Any import or export makes it a module.

options.option: Having the module keyword at the top of the file
Being listed in the tsconfig.json include array
Having a .ts extension rather than a .js extension

questions.question: What is the purpose of a .d.ts declaration file?
questions.answer: A .d.ts file contains only type declarations with no executable code. It lets TypeScript understand the types of an existing JavaScript library without having access to its source TypeScript files.
questions.hint: These ship alongside compiled .js files so consumers of a library get type information

questions.question: What is an @types package?

options.option: A community-maintained npm package of .d.ts files for an untyped JS library
options.comment: @types/lodash provides type declarations for lodash. TypeScript finds them automatically from node_modules/@types/.

options.option: A TypeScript built-in that provides types for Node.js standard modules
A package that generates TypeScript types from JSON at runtime
An npm scoped package that contains only TypeScript source files

questions.question: What does the declare keyword do in TypeScript?

options.option: Declares a type or variable that exists in the environment without emitting code
options.comment: declare const MY_API_URL: string tells TypeScript the variable exists globally without generating any JavaScript for it.

options.option: Makes a variable available globally across all files in the project
Declares that a module should not be bundled at compile time
Marks a class as abstract without using the abstract keyword

questions.question: How do you import only the type of a value in TypeScript to avoid runtime imports?
questions.answer: Use import type: import type { User } from './user'. This import is always erased at compile time — it never produces a runtime require or import statement.
questions.hint: Useful with isolatedModules: true where value imports that are only used as types would cause errors

questions.question: What is module augmentation in TypeScript?

options.option: Adding new types to an existing module using a declare module block
options.comment: declare module 'express' { interface Request { user?: User } } adds a user property to Express's Request type.

options.option: Importing a module and re-exporting its types under a different name
Merging two local module files into one compiled output file
Generating declaration files for a module using the --augment flag

questions.question: What is the difference between module: CommonJS and module: ESNext in tsconfig?
questions.answer: CommonJS emits require() and module.exports syntax for Node.js compatibility. ESNext emits ES module import/export syntax — used for browser bundles or modern Node.js with type: module in package.json.
questions.hint: Your runtime environment determines which one to use

questions.question: What is a triple-slash reference directive used for?

options.option: Including type declarations from another file or @types package explicitly
options.comment: /// <reference types="node" /> pulls in @types/node. /// <reference path="./globals.d.ts" /> includes a local declaration file.

options.option: Declaring that the current file is a module and not a global script
A comment directive to suppress TypeScript errors on the next line
Creating a compile-time import that is excluded from the output

questions.question: Why are TypeScript namespaces considered legacy in modern code?

options.option: ES modules now provide better module encapsulation without namespace pollution
options.comment: Namespaces were a pre-ESM solution for organising code. ES modules (import/export) are the standard — namespaces are only needed in .d.ts files for global augmentation.

options.option: Namespaces were removed in TypeScript 4.0 and cannot be used
Namespaces compile to eval() at runtime, making them unsafe
Namespaces conflict with JavaScript classes and cannot be used together

questions.question: How do you write a .d.ts file for an untyped JavaScript library that exports a single function?
questions.answer: declare module 'library-name' { export function doThing(input: string): number; export default doThing; }. Place it in a .d.ts file that TypeScript can find — either in the project or referenced in typeRoots.
questions.hint: The declare module block wraps all declarations for that library name

questions.question: What is a barrel file in a TypeScript project?

options.option: An index.ts file that re-exports items from multiple files for a cleaner public API
options.comment: export { Button } from './Button'; export { Input } from './Input'; in index.ts lets consumers import from the folder.

options.option: A file that bundles all TypeScript source files into one before compilation
A TypeScript config file that lists all module entry points
A .d.ts file that pre-declares all ambient variables for the project

questions.question: What does the package.json exports field control for a TypeScript library?

options.option: Which entry points are exposed and which module format they use
options.comment: exports maps subpaths to CJS/ESM files and their corresponding .d.ts files. Needed for dual CJS/ESM packages.

options.option: Which TypeScript version the library requires as a peer dependency
Which files the TypeScript compiler includes when processing the package
The list of TypeScript declaration files shipped with the package

questions.question: What is an ambient declaration file (globals.d.ts) and how does it differ from a module declaration file?
questions.answer: An ambient declaration file contains no import/export statements — its declare statements add to the global scope. A module declaration file (with declare module 'x') only adds types when that module is imported. Both use declare but one is global, the other is module-scoped.
questions.hint: A file without any import/export is treated as a script file — all its declarations become global

questions.question: How does TypeScript determine which .d.ts file to use when importing an npm package?

options.option: It checks the package's types or typings field in package.json first
options.comment: TypeScript reads the types field in package.json. If absent, it looks for index.d.ts. If still absent, it checks @types/ in node_modules.

options.option: It always uses the file at @types/{package-name} in node_modules
It searches for any .d.ts file inside the package folder recursively
It uses the main field from package.json and appends .d.ts automatically

questions.question: What is a type-only re-export and why does it matter with isolatedModules?

options.option: export type { X } re-exports only the type — erased at compile time safely
options.comment: With isolatedModules, re-exporting a type-only import as a value causes an error. export type { X } makes the intent clear.

options.option: A re-export that inlines the type declaration instead of referencing the source
An export that makes a value available only as a type, preventing instantiation
A compiler optimization that removes redundant type exports automatically

questions.question: What is declaration merging with a module augmentation for global scope?
questions.answer: Using declare global { } inside a module file lets you add declarations to the global scope. For example, adding a custom property to the Window interface: declare global { interface Window { myPlugin: MyPlugin; } }
questions.hint: The declare global block is needed inside a module file to escape the module scope into global scope

questions.question: What is a TypeScript decorator?

options.option: A special function that can annotate and modify classes, methods, or properties
options.comment: Decorators are applied with @name syntax above the target. They run at class definition time and can inspect or modify behavior.

options.option: A TypeScript comment syntax for documenting classes and methods
A design pattern that wraps objects with additional behavior at runtime
A configuration object passed to a class constructor for dependency injection

questions.question: What is the difference between Stage 3 decorators (TypeScript 5.0+) and the older experimental decorators?
questions.answer: Stage 3 decorators are the TC39 standard, enabled by default in TypeScript 5.0 without any flags. Experimental decorators required experimentalDecorators: true in tsconfig. They have different APIs and semantics and are not compatible.
questions.hint: You need experimentalDecorators: true only for the old Angular/NestJS style decorators

questions.question: What does the @Component decorator do in Angular?

options.option: Marks a class as an Angular component and provides its metadata (template, styles)
options.comment: @Component({ selector, template, styles }) registers the class as a component and links it to its view template.

options.option: Injects a service into the component class automatically
Registers the class as a singleton service in Angular's DI container
Compiles the component template and emits the HTML at build time

questions.question: What does the @Injectable() decorator do in both Angular and NestJS?

options.option: Marks a class as a provider that can be injected via the DI system
options.comment: In Angular, @Injectable({ providedIn: 'root' }) registers the service globally. In NestJS it marks a provider for module injection.

options.option: Creates a singleton instance of the class immediately at startup
Decorates an interface to make it usable as an injection token
Adds async error handling middleware to the decorated class

questions.question: How do you type React component props using TypeScript?
questions.answer: Define an interface or type alias for the props and use it as the generic parameter of React.FC or annotate the destructured parameter: function Button({ label }: { label: string }) or const Button: React.FC<ButtonProps> = ({ label }) => ...
questions.hint: React.FC<Props> is one way; directly annotating the parameter is another and is often preferred

questions.question: What is a decorator factory in TypeScript?

options.option: A function that accepts configuration and returns a decorator function
options.comment: @Component({ selector: 'app-root' }) is a factory — Component() returns the actual decorator. This allows parameterised decorators.

options.option: A class that creates and registers multiple decorators simultaneously
A utility function provided by TypeScript's core library for decorator creation
A pattern where one decorator applies other decorators inside it

questions.question: What does the @Controller('users') decorator do in NestJS?

options.option: Marks the class as a route controller and sets the base path to /users
options.comment: NestJS uses @Controller to register route handlers. Methods inside use @Get(), @Post() etc. to map HTTP methods to handlers.

options.option: Injects the UsersService into the controller via the DI container
Generates a CRUD module for the User entity automatically
Applies authentication middleware to all routes in the controller

questions.question: How do you type the useState hook in React with TypeScript?
questions.answer: Pass the type as a generic argument: const [count, setCount] = useState<number>(0). When the initial value makes the type unambiguous, TypeScript infers it: useState(0) infers number. Use the generic form when the initial value is null or undefined.
