questions.hint: useState<User | null>(null) is the typical pattern for initially empty state

questions.question: What is a class decorator in TypeScript Stage 3 syntax?

options.option: A function that receives the class constructor and can replace or modify it
options.comment: Stage 3 class decorators receive the class value. They can return a new class to replace the original or return nothing to just observe.

options.option: A function that runs before each method call on the class instance
A type-level annotation that has no runtime effect on the class
A decorator that automatically calls super() in the class constructor

questions.question: What does emitDecoratorMetadata: true enable in TypeScript?

options.option: Emits Reflect.metadata() calls with type information for decorator reflection
options.comment: emitDecoratorMetadata works with reflect-metadata to expose type annotations at runtime — used by Angular and NestJS DI.

options.option: Generates documentation files from decorator JSDoc comments
Enables the Decorator Metadata proposal (TC39 Stage 3) in TypeScript
Emits type definitions for all decorated classes to .d.ts files automatically

questions.question: How do you type a React useRef pointing to a DOM element?
questions.answer: const inputRef = useRef<HTMLInputElement>(null). The generic specifies the element type. Access the DOM node as inputRef.current — TypeScript knows it is HTMLInputElement | null.
questions.hint: Pass null as the initial value — the ref starts empty until React attaches it to the element

questions.question: What is the TC39 Decorator Metadata proposal supported in TypeScript 5.2?

options.option: A standard way to attach and read metadata on decorated class elements
options.comment: Decorator Metadata provides a built-in Symbol.metadata on each class. Decorators write to it; consumers read from it at runtime.

options.option: A proposal that adds runtime type information to all TypeScript types
A way to attach JSDoc metadata to TypeScript decorators for IDE tooling
An API that generates schema objects from decorated class properties

questions.question: How do you type a React Context with TypeScript?
questions.answer: const ThemeContext = createContext<string>('light'). The generic parameter sets the context value type. Use React.createContext<Type>(defaultValue) — the default value must match the type.
questions.hint: If no sensible default exists, use createContext<Type | null>(null) and handle null in consumers

questions.question: What is the purpose of the @Param() decorator in NestJS?

options.option: Extracts a named route parameter from the request URL into a method argument
options.comment: @Get(':id') with @Param('id') id: string extracts the :id segment from the URL path.

options.option: Validates and transforms query string parameters before injection
Injects a request body property into the method argument
Marks a method parameter as required by the OpenAPI specification

questions.question: How does Angular use TypeScript decorators and metadata for dependency injection?
questions.answer: Angular reads the constructor parameter types at runtime using Reflect.metadata (from emitDecoratorMetadata). It uses those types as injection tokens to resolve and inject services from its DI container automatically.
questions.hint: This is why Angular services must be classes — interfaces and primitives cannot be injection tokens via reflection

questions.question: What is a method decorator in TypeScript and what arguments does it receive?

options.option: A function receiving the class prototype, method name, and property descriptor
options.comment: Method decorators can modify the descriptor to intercept calls, add logging, retry logic, etc. Stage 3 slightly changes the API.

options.option: A function that receives the method arguments at every call site
A decorator applied to function parameters to transform their values
A function that receives the class constructor and the method's return type

questions.question: How do you type an Angular @Input() property that is required?

options.option: Use the required input signal: input.required<string>() in Angular 17+
options.comment: input.required<string>() in Angular 17+ gives a compile-time error if the parent does not pass the input. Older way: @Input({ required: true }) with strictPropertyInitialization.

options.option: @Input() name!: string — the definite assignment assertion makes it required
@Input({ required: true }) applies both type and runtime validation
Angular inputs cannot be typed as required — they are always optional

questions.question: What is the difference between typing React event handlers inline vs with React.ChangeEventHandler?
questions.answer: Inline: onChange={(e: React.ChangeEvent<HTMLInputElement>) => ...}. Using the type alias: const handler: React.ChangeEventHandler<HTMLInputElement> = (e) => ... lets TypeScript infer e without annotation. Both are equivalent — the type alias form is cleaner for named handlers.
questions.hint: React.ChangeEventHandler<T> is a type alias for (event: React.ChangeEvent<T>) => void

questions.question: What makes NestJS pipes useful in conjunction with TypeScript class-validator decorators?

options.option: They transform and validate incoming request payloads against typed DTO classes
options.comment: ValidationPipe + @IsString(), @IsEmail() on DTO class properties gives runtime validation that mirrors TypeScript types.

options.option: They inject the DI container directly into the decorated class properties
They generate OpenAPI schemas from TypeScript class decorator metadata
They compile TypeScript types to JSON Schema at application startup

questions.question: How do you type a custom React hook that returns a value and a setter?
questions.answer: Annotate the return type as a tuple: function useToggle(): [boolean, () => void] { const [on, setOn] = useState(false); return [on, () => setOn(v => !v)]; }. Use as const on the return value if TypeScript widens the tuple to an array.
questions.hint: Without the return type annotation TypeScript may infer (boolean | () => void)[] instead of a proper tuple

questions.question: What is the accessor keyword introduced with Stage 3 decorators in TypeScript 4.9?

options.option: Declares an auto-accessor that generates a getter/setter pair for decorators to intercept
options.comment: accessor name: string auto-generates a backing private field, get name(), and set name() — giving accessor decorators something to wrap.

options.option: An alternative to get and set keywords for defining property accessors
A modifier that makes a private field accessible to subclass decorators
A TypeScript-only keyword removed at compile time with no runtime equivalent
